metadata.subject: Arts Education
metadata.description: Art Studio 10 provides students with foundational visual arts experiences across a range of media and processes. Students develop technical skills in drawing, painting, and mixed media; explore the elements and principles of design intentionally; engage with art history from diverse cultural perspectives; and begin building a studio practice marked by inquiry, reflection, and original artistic voice. First Peoples visual art traditions are central to the curriculum. This course opens pathways to Studio Arts 2D, Studio Arts 3D, Photography, and Graphic Arts.
metadata.general_description: A foundational senior visual arts course developing technical skills across media (drawing, painting, printmaking, and mixed media), art history literacy, and original artistic voice — preparing students for specialized senior visual arts pathways.

big_ideas.statement: Creative growth requires patience, readiness to take risks, and willingness to try new approaches.
big_ideas.elaboration: Studio practice means embracing the iterative, often unpredictable nature of art-making — experimenting, failing, learning, and persisting. Growth comes from sustained engagement with the creative process, not just the final product.

big_ideas.statement: Visual arts are a unique language for creating and communicating.
big_ideas.elaboration: Visual artists communicate through the elements of art and principles of design — using line, colour, form, space, and composition to convey meaning, emotion, and perspective without words.

big_ideas.statement: Artists challenge established perspectives and expose us to new viewpoints.
big_ideas.elaboration: Throughout history, artists have used their work to question social norms, document injustice, celebrate beauty, and expand our collective vision of what is possible. Engaging with challenging art develops critical thinking and empathy.

big_ideas.statement: Individuals and communities express identity and culture through visual art.
big_ideas.elaboration: Visual art is a powerful form of cultural memory and identity expression. From Northwest Coast design to contemporary street art, visual traditions carry meaning that connects people to their heritage and community.

competencies.raw: Develop technical skills across media and processes; apply elements and principles of design intentionally; explore relationships between identity, place, and culture in studio work; take creative risks and experiment with new approaches.
i_can_statements: I can develop technical skills in drawing, painting, printmaking, and mixed media.
I can apply elements of art and principles of design intentionally to create meaningful compositions.
I can explore how my identity, community, and cultural background appear in and shape my studio work.
I can take creative risks and embrace experimentation as part of my artistic process.

competencies.raw: Analyze artworks using visual arts vocabulary; apply feedback to improve studio work; connect artworks to social, cultural, historical, and personal contexts; examine artists' intentions and processes.
i_can_statements: I can analyze artworks using accurate visual arts vocabulary: elements, principles, media, and style.
I can apply feedback from my teacher and peers to refine and strengthen my studio work.
I can connect artworks — including my own — to the social, cultural, and historical contexts in which they were made.
I can examine and explain an artist's intentions and creative decision-making process.

competencies.raw: Present and share studio work in appropriate contexts; use symbolism and visual language to convey meaning; document artistic process and growth through portfolios and artist statements.
i_can_statements: I can present and share my studio work appropriately for different audiences and contexts.
I can use visual symbolism, imagery, and composition to convey specific meaning and personal voice.
I can document my artistic process and growth through a portfolio and written artist statements.

competencies.raw: Demonstrate ethical responsibility in creating and sharing art; explore diverse art traditions including First Peoples visual arts; examine how art impacts culture and community; build connections through art.
i_can_statements: I can demonstrate ethical responsibility in art-making, including respecting copyright and avoiding cultural appropriation.
I can engage with First Peoples visual art traditions respectfully, understanding their cultural significance.
I can examine how visual art shapes and reflects cultural values, social issues, and community identity.

areas.name: Elements and Principles of Art and Design
areas.raw: Elements: line, shape, form, colour, value, texture, space; Principles: balance, contrast, emphasis, pattern, rhythm, unity, variety.
topics: Line: types, qualities, and expressive use
Shape and form: geometric vs. organic, positive/negative space
Colour theory: colour wheel, complementary, analogous, warm/cool, and colour mixing
Value: tonal range, shading techniques, and value scales
Texture: actual vs. implied, surface quality in 2D and 3D work
Principles of design: balance, contrast, emphasis, pattern, rhythm, unity, and variety
Compositional strategies: rule of thirds, visual flow, and focal point

areas.name: Drawing and Painting
areas.raw: Drawing and painting techniques and media.
topics: Observational drawing: still life, proportion, foreshortening, and gesture drawing
Pencil techniques: hatching, crosshatching, blending, and contour drawing
Painting media: watercolour, acrylic, and gouache — basic techniques and colour mixing
Figure drawing: gesture, proportion, and anatomical basics
Perspective drawing: one-point and two-point perspective

areas.name: Printmaking and Mixed Media
areas.raw: Printmaking processes; mixed media and collage.
topics: Relief printing: linocut, woodcut, and foam printing
Screen printing: basic stencil techniques
Collage and assemblage: combining materials, textures, and found objects
Mixed media: integrating drawing, painting, printmaking, and photography
Experimental approaches: resist techniques, mono-printing, and transfer processes

areas.name: Art History and Cultural Contexts
areas.raw: Art history from diverse cultures; First Peoples visual arts traditions.
topics: Overview of Western art history: Impressionism, Expressionism, Modernism, and Contemporary art
First Peoples visual arts of BC: form lines, ovoids, formline design, and Northwest Coast tradition
Global art traditions: African, Asian, Indigenous, and Latin American visual arts
Contemporary Indigenous artists: learning from living First Peoples artists
Contextual analysis: understanding art in its social, political, and cultural context
Cultural appropriation in visual arts: understanding and respecting boundaries
